title: Student Welfare: Crisis Intervention
description: Conroe ISD has procedures in place to identify and support students who may be experiencing a personal crisis, such as a mental health emergency or thoughts of self-harm. The district works to connect these students with appropriate care and resources as quickly as possible. Parents and community members can rely on these guidelines to understand how the school responds when a student's safety or well-being is at serious risk.

timeline_local.summary: This update to Conroe ISD's FFB (Crisis Intervention) Local Policy substantially expands and restructures the threat assessment framework, adding provisions for multi-campus teams, parental notification before assessments, written parental consent for mental health services, special education student protections, record retention requirements, and clarifying training provider language, while modernizing terminology throughout (e.g., replacing "substance abuse program" with "substance use prevention and intervention").
changes_added: A team may serve more than one campus of the District, provided that: 1. Each District campus is assigned a team; and 2. In serving a particular campus, the team includes the person designated to serve as the campus behavior coordinator for that campus.
this Policy, and the District multi-hazard operational plan (added to list of team expertise areas)
The Superintendent shall adopt procedures to implement this Policy and Texas law, consistent with the model policies and procedures developed by the Texas School Safety Center.

Notice of a Threat section: The Superintendent shall require that, as soon as safe and practicable after an administrator or team for a District campus receives information regarding a threat made against that campus, including through social media, the administrator or team immediately provide to each member of the teaching staff, including teacher's aides, who may be directly affected by the threat notice that includes: (1) a statement of the existence of the threat; (2) the nature of the threat; and (3) any other pertinent details to ensure student and staff safety.
the Texas School Safety Center or a regional education service center regarding evidence-based threat assessment programs.
to the team (in Employee Confidentiality section)

for the team to assess and report individuals who make threats of violence or exhibit harmful, threatening, or violent behavior, gathering and analyzing data to determine the level of risk and appropriate intervention, including: 1. referring a student for mental health assessment; and 2. implementing an escalation procedure, if appropriate based on the team's assessment, in accordance with District policy.
making (replacing prior word in threat assessment process step 1)
the team or designee will (in step 2 pre-assessment language)
Notify (replacing 'Notifying' — grammatical correction to match 'will')
Provide (replacing 'Provideing' — grammatical correction, step 2b)
Provide (replacing 'Provideing' — grammatical correction, step 2c)

If a student in a special education program is the subject of a threat assessment, the team conducting the threat assessment must include at least one of the following persons who has specific knowledge of the student's disability and the disability's manifestations: (1) a special education teacher who provides instruction to the student; (2) a behavior analyst licensed under Chapter 506, Occupations Code; (3) a clinical or master social worker licensed under Chapter 505, Occupations Code; or (4) a specialist in school psychology licensed under Chapter 501, Occupations Code. The team may also include: (1) an educational diagnostician; (2) a behavior specialist.
if the individual is a student (qualifying language in serious-risk-of-violence reporting requirement)
This requirement does not prevent an employee of the school from acting immediately to prevent an imminent threat or respond to an emergency.

A team may not provide a mental health care service to a student who is under 18 years of age unless the team obtains written consent from the parent of or person standing in parental relation to the student before providing the mental health care service. The consent required must be submitted on a form developed by the District that complies with all applicable state and federal law. The student's parent or person standing in parental relation to the student may give consent for a student to receive ongoing services or may limit consent to one or more services provided on a single occasion.

Materials and information provided to or produced by a team during a threat assessment of a student under this section must be maintained in the student's school record until the student's 24th birthday.

Additionally, the Superintendent shall coordinate with law enforcement authorities as necessary and take other appropriate action in accordance with the District's multi-hazard emergency operations plan.
procedures related to substance use prevention and intervention.
to the community, school, or individual and support the District in implementing the District's multihazard emergency operations plan
changes_removed: an approved provider on evidence-based threat assessment programs.
to another person, the campus, or the community

The redline is Version 1, suggesting this is a new local policy being established. Red underlined and red strikethrough formatting was clearly distinguishable. Several additions involve grammatical corrections (Notifying→Notify, Provideing→Provide) that appear as tracked changes. The Notice of a Threat section header and its full text are entirely red-underlined, indicating it is a new section addition.

timeline_local.summary: This update to Conroe ISD's FFB (Student Welfare: Crisis Intervention) policy adds specificity to the qualifications required for threat assessment team members, allows a single team to serve multiple campuses under defined conditions, and introduces new requirements for threat assessments involving students in special education programs, including the mandatory inclusion of at least one qualified special education professional on the assessment team.
changes_added: who have expertise in counseling, behavior management, mental health and substance use, classroom instruction, special education, school administration, school safety and security, emergency management, and law enforcement
A team may serve more than one campus of a school district, provided that:
Each district campus is assigned a team; and
In serving a particular campus, the team includes the person designated to serve as the campus behavior coordinator for that campus.

If a student in a special education program, as defined by law, is the subject of a threat assessment, the team conducting the threat assessment must include at least one of the following persons who has specific knowledge of the student's disability and the disability's manifestations:
A special education teacher who provides instruction to the student;
A Licensed Behavior Analyst;
An educational diagnostician;
A behavior specialist;
A Licensed Clinical Social Worker or a Licensed Master Social Worker; or
A Licensed School Psychologist.
timeline_local.notes: The cover page does not contain a large bold heading indicating a specific board action (e.g., "Discuss Revisions," "Adopt Revisions," etc.) — it only shows the policy title and metadata. Action has been marked as 'unknown' because the action type cannot be determined from the available cover page content. All red underlined text has been captured; no red strikethrough text was identified in the document.

timeline_local.summary: This update to Conroe ISD's FFB (Local) Crisis Intervention policy strengthens the threat assessment process by requiring team member expertise in specific areas, mandating parental notification and participation before and after assessments, and clarifying protocols for students at risk of suicide who also make threats of violence. It also updates substance-related language from "having a substance abuse issue" to "using or possessing tobacco, drugs, or alcohol."
changes_added: The Superintendent of the district shall ensure, to the greatest extent practicable, that the members appointed to each team have expertise in counseling, behavior management, mental health and substance use, classroom instruction, special education, school administration, school safety and security, emergency management, and law enforcement.

Before an assessment takes place: a. Notifying the parent of or person standing in parental relation to the student regarding the assessment. b. Providing an opportunity for the parent of or person standing in parental relation to the student to participate in the assessment, either in person or remotely. c. Providing an opportunity for the parent of or person standing in parental relation to the student to submit to the team information regarding the student.
After completing a threat assessment of a student, providing to the parent of or person standing in parental relation to the student the team's findings and conclusions regarding the student.

If the student at risk of suicide also makes a threat of violence to others, the team shall conduct a threat assessment in addition to actions taken in accordance with the district's suicide prevention program.
using or possessing tobacco, drugs, or alcohol

description_local: This policy governs how the school district responds to crisis situations involving students, including threats to safety, and outlines the teams and procedures put in place to assess and address those situations. It applies to students, school staff, and the broader school community who may be affected by a crisis or safety concern. Parents and community members should be aware of this policy because it directly shapes how the district identifies, evaluates, and responds to potential threats, helping to ensure that schools remain safe and supportive environments for all students.
